content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COM1vnssIoN
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916)322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
October 13, 1998
Jeannie M. Mabry
Council Assistant
Chris Mathys, Councilmember
City of Fresno
2600 Fresno Street
Fresno, California 93721
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-230
Dear Ms. Mabry:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf ofDeWayne Zinkin
regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
May Mr. Zinkin pay for the cost of a mass mailing, featuring a councilmember, that urges
the city council to build a public works project?
CONCLUSION
As long as the city does not assist in the mailing, nothing in the Act prohibits an
individual from funding a mass mailing relating to a community issue. Further, as long as the
mass mailing contains no express advocacy, no reference to the councilmember’s candidacy, and
does not solicit contributions, it would not be considered a contribution and no reportable
contribution will result.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Con-,niission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-230
Page 2
FACTS
You faxed me a letter dated September 14, 1998, which states that Councilmember
Mathys is authorized to request advice on behalf of Mr. Zinkin. You also furnished me with a
copy of the proposed flyer on September 24, 1998.
In a conversation with you on October 9, 1998, you notified me that Councilmember
Mathys will not be on the November 1998 ballot as his term does not end until January 2001.
You also indicated to me that the city is not assisting with the producing or mailing of this flyer.
The production of the flyer will be handled by Orman Communications and a separate entity will
be doing the mailing. Mr. Zinkin will determine which company will mall the flyers. The only
information you provided to Orman Communications is the facts pertaining to the project, which
is information that is available to any member of public.
DeWayne Zinicin is a developer in the City of Fresno (the “city”). A large railroad
underpass project will affect traffic flow on several developments owned by Mr. Zinkin. This
project will affect a large volume oftraffic and will provide more accessibility to these existing
developments. Due to the significance ofthis project, Mr. Zinicin has offered financial assistance
to distribute a mass mailer notifying the city residents ofthis proposed public works project. The
flyer would specifically include a councilmember’s photograph, and his name will be mentioned
several times. The purpose of the flyer is to generate attendance by residents at the council
meeting when the vote on this project will take place.
ANALYSIS
Your general question implicates issues regarding the mass mailing provisions of the Act.
Also, a contribution to the councilmember may result from the payments made by an individual
publicizing the meeting, depending on the factual situation. (Section 82015.) Contributions are
reportable under the Act. Further, many jurisdictions limit contributions, as “contribution” is
defined under the Act.
I. In-kind Contribution of Free Publicity
Under certain circumstances, payments made at the behest of a public official are
contributions to the public official. (Section 82015.)
“Made at the behest of means made
under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of.” (Regulation
18225.7.)


File No. A-98-230
Page 3
Even if a payment is a contribution to the councilmember as discussed above, a payment
which is for a communication may fall within an exception to the definition of contribution.
2
(See Regulation 18215, copy attached.) Regulation 1821 5(c)(4) provides a pertinent exception:
“(4) A payment made at the behest of a candidate, which is for a
communication by the candidate or any other person, that meets all
of the following:
(i) Does not contain express advocacy;
(ii) Does not make reference to the candidate ‘s candidacyfor
elective office, the candidate’s election campaign, or the
candidate’s or his or her opponent’s qualifications for office; and
(iii) Does not solicit contributions to the candidate or to third
persons for use in support of the candidate or in opposition to the
candidate’s opponent.” (Emphasis added.)
Therefore, if the flyer is sent by Mr. Zinkin at the behest of the councilmember, but meets
the criteria above, no contribution will result. Also, no reporting obligation will result if this
exception is met. Please keep in mind that, if a flyer contained express advocacy and language
referring to the candidate’s candidacy for elective office, and is made at the behest ofthe
councilmember, it will be considered a contribution to the councilmember. (Regulation 18215.)
In addition, if the flyer contains express advocacy or language referring to the candidate’s
candidacy and is not prepared in coordination with the councilmember, but rather independently
by the sender, the sender will have to report the flyer as an independent expenditure. (Section
82031.)
The term “express advocacy” is defined in Regulation 18225(b)(2) (copy attached) and
reads as follows:
“A communication ‘expressly advocates’ the nomination,
election or defeat of a candidate or the qualification, passage or
defeat of a measure if it contains express words of advocacy such
as ‘vote for,’ ‘elect,’ ‘support,’ ‘cast your ballot,’ ‘vote against,’
‘defeat,’ ‘reject,’ ‘sign petitions for’ or otherwise refers to a clearly
identified candidate or measure so that the communication, taken
as a whole, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election.”
(Regulation 1 8225(b)(2).)
2 Please note that the defmition of a contribution itself, Section 82015, has been recently amended to
exclude certain payments that are made at the behest of a candidate from the defmition of contribution. Also, while
certain payments are no longer considered “contributions,” those same payments may nonetheless be reportable
according to the amended definition of contribution.


File No. A-98-230
Page 4
The proposed flyer that you faxed to me on September 24, 1998, does not contain express
advocacy, does not contain language referring to the candidate’s candidacy for elective office and
does not solicit contributions.
H. Mass Mailings
Section 89001 states:
“No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public
expense.”
You have indicated to me that the city is not assisting with the producing or mailing of
the flyer. Therefore, Regulation 18901 does not apply and nothing in the Act prohibits
Mr. Zinkin from funding this mass mailing and no reportable contribution will result.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant, Legal Division
SGC:LD:tls
Enclosures
